title: Pyrography mandala on a round birch panel
subtitle: A geometric mandala burned onto a 25 cm birch plywood disc
excerpt: A geometric mandala burned onto a 25 cm birch plywood disc: the design is divided into eight equal segments using compass and ruler, then transferred to the wood and burned section by section. The project covers design transfer, outline burning with a writing tip, and progressive shading with a shading tip to build tonal depth.
sourceNotes: Victorian pyrography manuals and pokerwork guides; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for decorative wood burning.

content.text: A pyrography tip at working temperature can cause a serious burn in under a second. Return the burner to its cooling stand between every stroke. Never rest the burner on the bench surface or on fabric.

A mandala design suits pyrography. The repeating geometry lets you finish one section fully before burning the next. Each completed segment teaches you the heat shade range of the burner. The round birch disc is also a forgiving surface. The pale even grain of birch ply burns to a uniform tone, without the busy figure of solid timber.
One birch plywood disc, 250 mm diameter and 6 mm thick, sanded to 240-grit on the top face. Tracing paper for design transfer. A compass and ruler for layout.

Mark the centre of the disc with a light pencil dot. Draw six to eight concentric circles at 15 mm intervals from the centre. Divide the disc into eight equal segments by drawing four diameter lines through the centre at 45-degree intervals. The grid of circles and radii creates the cells of the mandala design. Sketch the pattern into the cells lightly in pencil before committing to the burner.

Fit the writing tip. Warm the burner fully before touching the wood; a tip that has not reached working temperature will drag rather than burn cleanly. Work one segment at a time: burn the outlines of that segment completely before moving to the next. This allows the completed segment to act as a tonal reference for the rest of the disc. Move the tip at a steady pace: faster for light lines, slower for darker ones.
Switch to the shading tip. Work the dark areas of each cell first using long smooth strokes with the grain. Build tone gradually; it is easier to darken an area than to lighten one. Use
for transitional mid-tones at the edge of shaded areas, where a smooth gradient reads more naturally than a hard line.

Erase any remaining pencil lines with a soft eraser after the burning is complete; the eraser will not affect the burned surface. Apply one light coat of wax or matte lacquer to the surface to protect the burned design and even the tone of the unburned birch.

items.fix: Move the shading tip at a slow, even pace and always complete the stroke in a single continuous movement. Overlap each stroke by one-third with the previous one, working across the area in one direction only.
items.cause: Shading tip moved too quickly over the surface, or strokes stopped mid-line leaving a lighter mark at the end of each stroke
items.symptom: Shaded areas look patchy with visible unburned streaks
